---
number-sections: false
---

# APPENDICES {#sec-annexos}

This appendix gathers supporting material for the main body of the work, organized into five blocks:

- **A.I** Tables of parameters and variables of the Orsi, Raggi, and Turino (2014) model, to facilitate reading of @sec-orsi-turino.
- **A.II** Full diagnostics of the estimation on simulated data: prior-versus-posterior plots, traceplots, and corner plots (@sec-simulat).
- **A.III** Full diagnostics of the estimation on Spanish data: prior versus posterior, traceplots, and corner plots (@sec-real).
- **A.IV** Complementary impulse-response functions not included in @sec-analisis for space reasons.
- **A.V** Laffer curves for the four fiscal policy instruments analyzed in @sec-analisis.

## Parameters and Variables of the Orsi-Raggi-Turino Model {#sec-annex-orsi}

### Structural Parameters ($\texorpdfstring{\Theta}{Theta}$)

::: {#tbl-orsi-params tbl-colwidths="[10,67,20]"}
|  | Description | Range/Restriction |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| $\beta$ | Subjective discount factor | $(0, 1)$ |
| $\sigma$ | Inverse of the intertemporal elasticity of substitution | $\sigma > 0$ |
| $\gamma$ | Growth rate of technological progress ($\Gamma_t = \gamma \Gamma_{t-1}$) | $\gamma > 1$ |
| $\alpha, \alpha_u$ | Labor elasticity in the regular and underground sectors | $(0, 1)$ |
| $\delta_k$ | Capital depreciation rate | $(0, 1)$ |
| $\xi, \phi$ | Inverse of labor supply elasticity (total hours and informal-specific, respectively) | $> 0$ |
| $B_0$ | Weight of total labor disutility (formal + informal) | $B_0 > 0$ |
| $B_1$ | Additional disutility cost specific to informal labor | $B_1 \ge 0$ |
| $p$ | Mean stochastic probability of being inspected | $(0, 1)$ |
| $s$ | Legal penalty or surcharge on evaded corporate tax | $s > 1$ |

: Structural Parameters of the Orsi et al. Model
:::

### System Variables

| Type |  | Description |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **State Variables** | $k_t$ | Total capital stock at the start of the period |
| | $A_t, B_t$ | Structural productivity shocks |
| **Flow / Control Variables** | $y_t, y^m_t, y^u_t$ | Output (total, regular, and underground) |
| | $c_t, i_t$ | Private consumption and aggregate gross investment |
| | $n^m_t, n^u_t$ | Hours worked (formal and informal) |
| **Fiscal / Policy Variables** | $\tau^c_t, \tau^s_t, \tau^h_t$ | Tax rates (corporate, social security, income) |
| | $p_t$ | Monitoring effort or inspection probability |
| **Fiscal Revenue** | $G^h_t, G^c_t, G^s_t$ | Personal income tax, corporate tax, and social security contributions |
: Classification of Model Variables {#tbl-orsi-vars tbl-colwidths="[18,10,68]"}

## Diagnostics of the Simulated Estimation {#sec-annex-sim}

### Prior-versus-Posterior Plots

![Prior vs. Posterior — Structural parameters (T=116)](../outputs/orsi/distr_estructurals_sim.png){#fig-distr-est-sim}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![Prior vs. Posterior — AR(1) persistences (T=116)](../outputs/orsi/distr_persistencies_sim.png){#fig-distr-per-sim}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![Prior vs. Posterior — Shock standard deviations (T=116)](../outputs/orsi/distr_shocks_sim.png){#fig-distr-shk-sim}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

### Traceplots

![Traceplots — Structural parameters (T=116)](../outputs/orsi/trace_estructurals_sim.png){#fig-trace-est-sim}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![Traceplots — AR(1) persistences (T=116)](../outputs/orsi/trace_persistencies_sim.png){#fig-trace-per-sim}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![Traceplots — Shock standard deviations (T=116)](../outputs/orsi/trace_shocks_sim.png){#fig-trace-shk-sim}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

### Corner Plots

![Corner plot — Structural parameters (T=116)](../outputs/orsi/corner_estructurals_sim.png){#fig-corner-est-sim}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![Corner plot — AR(1) persistences (T=116)](../outputs/orsi/corner_persistencies_sim.png){#fig-corner-per-sim}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![Corner plot — Shock standard deviations (T=116)](../outputs/orsi/corner_shocks_sim.png){#fig-corner-shk-sim}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

## Spanish Data: Sources and Transformations {#sec-annex-dades}

| Series | Source and filters |
|:---|:---|
| Private consumption | Eurostat NAMQ\_10\_GDP · indicator P3 · CLV 2010 M€ · SCA · Spain |
| Gross investment | Eurostat NAMQ\_10\_GDP · indicator P51G · CLV 2010 M€ · SCA · Spain |
| Employee compensation (D1) | Eurostat NAMQ\_10\_GDP · indicator D1 · CP M€ · SCA · Spain |
| Corporate tax revenue | IGAE, Quarterly National Accounts, Non-financial Transactions of General Government |
| Personal income tax revenue | IGAE, Quarterly National Accounts, Non-financial Transactions of General Government |
| Nominal GDP (implicit deflator) | Eurostat NAMQ\_10\_GDP · indicator B1GQ · CP M€ · SCA · Spain |
| Population 15–64 (per capita normalization) | Eurostat DEMO\_PJANBROAD · age group Y15-64 · annual · Spain |
: Sources of the Spanish data used in the estimation {#tbl-dades}

### Time Evolution of the Observable Series

![Observable series in real per capita levels (base 100 = 1996-Q1, left) and in first log-difference ×100 (right). The dotted lines indicate the 100 base and zero, respectively.](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_post/observable_pairs.png){#fig-obs-pairs}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

## Diagnostics of the Estimation with Real Data {#sec-annex-real}

### Prior-versus-Posterior Plots

![Prior vs. Posterior — Structural parameters (Spain, 1996–2019)](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_3000iter/distr_estructurals_real.png){#fig-distr-est-real}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![Prior vs. Posterior — AR(1) persistences (Spain, 1996–2019)](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_3000iter/distr_persistencies_real.png){#fig-distr-per-real}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![Prior vs. Posterior — Shock standard deviations (Spain, 1996–2019)](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_3000iter/distr_shocks_real.png){#fig-distr-shk-real}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

### Traceplots

![Traceplots — Structural parameters (Spain, 1996–2019)](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_3000iter/trace_estructurals_real.png){#fig-trace-est-real}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![Traceplots — AR(1) persistences (Spain, 1996–2019)](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_3000iter/trace_persistencies_real.png){#fig-trace-per-real}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![Traceplots — Shock standard deviations (Spain, 1996–2019)](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_3000iter/trace_shocks_real.png){#fig-trace-shk-real}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

### Corner Plots

![Corner plot — Structural parameters (Spain, 1996–2019)](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_3000iter/corner_estructurals_real.png){#fig-corner-est-real}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![Corner plot — AR(1) persistences (Spain, 1996–2019)](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_3000iter/corner_persistencies_real.png){#fig-corner-per-real}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![Corner plot — Shock standard deviations (Spain, 1996–2019)](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_3000iter/corner_shocks_real.png){#fig-corner-shk-real}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

## Impulse-Response Functions: Complementary Shocks {#sec-annex-irf}

![IRF to a positive personal income tax shock ($\tau^h_t$, +1σ). The magnitudes on output (−0.15 pp) and the underground share (+0.2 pp) are roughly half those of corporate tax, reflecting a labor channel less intense than the capital channel.](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_post/irf_eps_h.png){#fig-irf-h}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![IRF to a positive inspection probability shock ($p_t$, +1σ). The multipliers on all macroeconomic variables are on the order of $10^{-6}$, a consequence of $\sigma^2_p$ collapsing to the prior's lower bound.](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_post/irf_eps_p.png){#fig-irf-p}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![IRF to a positive intertemporal preference factor shock ($B_t$, +1σ). It acts mainly on consumption and saving; its effect on the underground share is practically nil (it explains less than 1.8% of the variance of the underground share).](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_post/irf_eps_b.png){#fig-irf-b}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![IRF to a positive investment adjustment cost shock ($\xi^x_t$, +1σ).](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_post/irf_eps_x.png){#fig-irf-x}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![IRF to a positive labor preference shock ($\xi^h_t$, +1σ).](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_post/irf_eps_xi_h.png){#fig-irf-xi-h}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

## Laffer Curves {#sec-annex-laffer}

![Laffer curve for corporate tax ($\tau^c$). Baseline: $\hat{\tau}^c_{ss} = 0.25$.](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_post/laffer_tau_c_ss.png){#fig-annex-laffer-tauc}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![Laffer curve for personal income tax ($\tau^h$). Baseline: $\hat{\tau}^h_{ss} = 0.23$.](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_post/laffer_tau_h_ss.png){#fig-annex-laffer-tauh}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![Laffer curve for social security contributions ($\tau^s$). Baseline: $\hat{\tau}^s_{ss} = 0.23$.](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_post/laffer_tau_s_ss.png){#fig-annex-laffer-taus}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

![Laffer curve for the inspection probability ($p$). Baseline: $\hat{p}_{ss} = 0.026$.](../outputs/orsi/real_no_covid_post/laffer_p_ss.png){#fig-annex-laffer-p}

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

## Transparency Appendix

In compliance with the principles of transparency and academic integrity, this appendix details the use of artificial intelligence (AI) language models during the development of this Bachelor's Thesis. The tool has been integrated mainly as a technical and analytical support instrument.

On one hand, it has been used as a programming assistant. This use was limited to improving computational efficiency and to using the `Julia` language, which was unfamiliar to me before starting this work. The proposed code was reviewed at every step, and the logic of the generated programs was understood.

On the other hand, the AI has played the role of an analytical interlocutor to discuss the economic interpretation of the results and the model's structural dynamics. This interaction was approached from a highly skeptical stance, demanding complete justifications in order to correctly understand the dynamics of the economic model. Furthermore, at certain points the arguments of two independent AI models were pitted against each other. This simulated "debate" exercise helped clarify concepts when a single AI did not provide a sufficiently satisfactory answer.

At all times, a critical and analytical mindset was maintained, avoiding the acceptance of answers without a complete understanding of the economic and statistical context.

## Code

All implementation code can be found in the GitHub repository: [https://github.com/Humadro/TFG-dsge-underground-economy-spain]. It contains several files: the model definition (`code/orsi.jl`), data generation and loading (`code/load_real_data_orsi.jl`, `code/simulate_data_orsi.jl`), the Bayesian estimation on simulated and real data (`code/bayes/bayesian_estimation_orsi_sim.jl`, `code/bayes/bayesian_estimation_orsi_real.jl`), steady-state verification (`code/check_steady_state.jl`), and the post-estimation analysis (`code/post_analisis/post_analisis.jl`, `code/post_analisis/counterfactuals.jl`), which includes the impulse-response functions, the variance and historical decompositions, the Laffer curves, and the counterfactual analysis.
